note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a history of a knee injury sustained while skiing in Vermont last weekend. She caught her ski and fell, resulting in a popping sensation in her knee. Since the injury, she has experienced pain and instability in the knee, particularly on the lateral aspect. She is unable to bear weight and has a decreased range of motion, with pain on palpation of the lateral knee and a positive Lachman sign. She has been taking ibuprofen for pain management, which has provided some relief.

Past medical history: The patient has no known medical conditions, no allergies, and no history of substance abuse. She is otherwise generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other chronic conditions.

Review of systems: There are no additional symptoms reported in other organs.

Current medications: Ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain on palpation of the lateral knee. There is decreased flexion and extension of the right knee, a positive Lachman sign, and palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse. There is no leg edema in the ankle.

Test Results: Not mentioned.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee, which is causing pain and instability.

Problem list: ACL rupture; knee pain; knee instability; knee effusion.

Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis and rule out any other damage to surrounding structures. I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain management. I recommended that the patient continue to use a brace to support the knee. I will see the patient again soon to discuss the results of the MRI and plan for further treatment.

Follow-up: I will see the patient again soon to discuss the results of the MRI and plan for further treatment.
